publications.title: A structural basis for the selection of dominant alphabeta T cell receptors in antiviral immunity.
publications.authors: Kjer-Nielsen L, Clements CS, Purcell AW, Brooks AG, Whisstock JC, Burrows SR, McCluskey J, Rossjohn J.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined an immunodominant αβ TCR recognizing an Epstein-Barr virus epitope presented on HLA class I, revealing how specific germline-encoded residues confer antigen specificity. Upon binding the pMHC complex, the TCR undergoes substantial conformational remodeling in its complementarity-determining regions, including disruption of canonical CDR1α and CDR2α structures to achieve optimal fit with the HLA-peptide surface. The findings establish a structural basis for TCR public clonotype selection in antiviral responses, showing that immunodominant TCR usage correlates with intrinsic structural properties that enable both high-affinity recognition and efficient conformational signaling through CD3 engagement, thereby linking TCR ligation mechanics to downstream intracellular activation pathways.

publications.title: T cell allorecognition via molecular mimicry.
publications.authors: Macdonald WA, Chen Z, Gras S, Archbold JK, Tynan FE, Clements CS, Bharadwaj M, Kjer-Nielsen L, Saunders PM, Wilce MC, Crawford F, Stadinsky B, Jackson D, Brooks AG, Purcell AW, Kappler JW, Burrows SR, Rossjohn J, McCluskey J.
publications.executive_summary: The LC13 T cell receptor, originally selected for recognition of self-HLA-B*0801 presenting a viral peptide, was found to cross-reactively alloreact against two HLA-B44 allotypes (HLA-B*4402 and HLA-B*4405) bound to distinct allopeptides through molecular mimicry. Despite the extensive polymorphism among these HLA alleles and the sequence divergence of the viral and allopeptides, the TCR engaged all three pHLA complexes with identical docking geometry; the viral and allopeptides adopted similar conformations only upon TCR binding, demonstrating an induced-fit mechanism of mimicry. Notably, LC13 cells did not alloreact against the closely related HLA-B*4403 allotype, and a single HLA polymorphism between HLA-B*4402 and HLA-B*4403 affected allopeptide plasticity, showing that mimicry capacity depends on both HLA and peptide identity. This work reveals that molecular mimicry can drive alloreactivity between structurally disparate cognate and allogeneic pHLA pairs, with implications for understanding TCR cross-reactivity in transplant rejection and autoimmunity where structural similarity across HLA variants may predict problematic TCR responses.
